title: How Innocent People Can Be Convicted
summary: Wrongful convictions happen when fallible evidence, institutional pressure, weak defense, misconduct, and human bias align inside a process designed to look authoritative.

lesson.learn: Wrongful convictions rarely come from one dramatic mistake. They usually form through a chain of ordinary-looking failures: an eyewitness becomes more certain after a suggestive lineup, a suspect confesses after exhaustion or pressure, a forensic method is overstated, a jailhouse informant is rewarded, a defense lawyer lacks resources, or prosecutors fail to disclose evidence that points away from guilt. Each step can seem plausible in isolation, which is why the final conviction may look solid even when the foundation is cracked. DNA exonerations exposed this pattern by showing that confident testimony and courtroom certainty can be wrong. The deeper lesson is structural: criminal procedure depends on people making judgments under stress, incentives, time pressure, and limited information. Safeguards such as recorded interrogations, blind lineups, open discovery, forensic standards, and post-conviction review matter because they reduce the chance that one error becomes irreversible.
lesson.simpler: Wrongful convictions happen when bad evidence and bad incentives reinforce each other. A mistaken ID, false confession, weak forensic claim, or hidden evidence can snowball into a conviction that feels certain but is not.
lesson.keyTakeaway: A wrongful conviction is usually a system failure: several human, scientific, and procedural errors align until doubt is converted into official certainty.
lesson.deeperDive: The most dangerous feature of wrongful convictions is confirmation bias. Once investigators settle on a suspect, later evidence may be interpreted through that assumption. Ambiguous forensic results become stronger than they are; witness memory hardens after feedback; alternative suspects receive less attention. Reform therefore focuses on procedure before trial, not only appeals after trial. Blind lineup administration, better forensic validation, defense funding, conviction-integrity units, and databases of exonerations all aim to catch errors earlier. Even then, exoneration is difficult because courts value finality and because new evidence often appears years after memories, records, and witnesses have faded.
lesson.funFact: DNA testing has freed people decades after conviction, but most criminal cases have no DNA evidence at all.

people: Barry Scheck - co-founder of the Innocence Project
Peter Neufeld - co-founder of the Innocence Project
Samuel Gross - co-founder of the National Registry of Exonerations
places: Innocence Project - legal organization focused on post-conviction innocence claims
National Registry of Exonerations - database of known U.S. exonerations
Trial courts - where evidence is tested before a jury or judge
concepts: Eyewitness misidentification - mistaken identification by a witness
False confession - admission to a crime by an innocent person
Brady disclosure - prosecution duty to reveal favorable evidence
Forensic validation - testing whether a method reliably supports its claims
